title: Stevens backs off ANWR plan
summary: Washington -- U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens called last week's Arctic drilling vote "a severe defeat" and said Tuesday that he won't proceed with an amendment that would have allowed drilling on Native lands within the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. "There's some other things we could have done on the coastal plain, but I don't see any reason to do that now," he said. Instead, his ANWR hopes are pinned on a House-Senate conference committee. That's where the Senate energy bill will be merged with the House version, which already includes ANWR drilling. "There's going to be a lot of horse-trading going on in that conference," Stevens said. Meanwhile, U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski won Senate approval of an Alaska gas line tax credit. That amendment, now part of the pending energy bill, is aimed at spu…

title: House pushes gas-line tax breaks
summary: Juneau -- A measure giving up to $500 million in tax breaks for construction of a natural gas pipeline is moving quickly through the House. Rep. Pete Kott, R-Eagle River, is sponsoring the bill, which would exempt a natural gas pipeline from local and state property taxes while it is being built and for the first two years of operation. It would also exempt it from sales taxes. Supporters of the bill say the tax breaks are needed for a gas pipeline from the North Slope to be built. But state officials characterize it as a giveaway that should first be justified through negotiations with the industry. Kott argued Monday the tax break is warranted. "We don't have a viable project now to tax, so we're not losing anything," he told the House Resources Committee. "There's perhaps some short-ter…

title: Rabies epidemic confirmed in northern Alaska
summary: State epidemiologists say there is a rabies epizootic, an epidemic among animals, in northern Alaska. Since Oct. 1, the state virology laboratory in Fairbanks has tested 166 animal carcasses from rural villages. Microbiologists determined that 66 animals, a fraction under 40 percent, tested positive for rabies, said lab manager Don Ritter. Microbiologists found rabies in 44 arctic foxes, 18 red foxes and four dogs. Most were from the North Slope, but animals from Western and Southwest Alaska also tested positive. Exposure has not been limited to animals. So far 15 people, mostly in Nuiqsut, a village about 160 miles east of Barrow, have been exposed, most likely through a bite that broke the skin. "What you need to have contact with is an animal's saliva," said Dr. Louisa Castrodale, a sta…

title: Crew drops groomer in emergency
summary: A sudden, dangerous whiteout in the mountains north of Girdwood forced the crew of an Army National Guard helicopter to drop a 3-ton snow groomer Wednesday evening while in flight to a ski training center on Eagle Glacier. The four-member crew was unhurt and landed safely. But the $32,000 snow cat plunged about 140 feet to the glacier and appeared to be seriously crunched if not totaled. "It's very badly beat up. Some of the wheels are broken off, and the cab is virtually demolished," said Jim Galanes, head coach of Alaska Pacific University's Nordic Ski Center. "But whatever happened, whatever went wrong, (the pilot) did the right thing. I have no qualms about that at all." The UH-60L Black Hawk was hauling the stripped-down cat from the Girdwood airport to APU's Thomas Training Center wh…

title: Students face off on ocean sciences
summary: SEWARD -- Some of the smartest high school students in Alaska will engage in a battle of the brains this weekend in Seward, and the winning team will go to the East Coast in April to compete for a national prize. The fifth annual National Ocean Sciences Bowl Alaska regional tournament starts this morning and continues through Sunday. Eleven teams from all over Alaska, including squads from Dimond and Chugiak high schools, will compete. Each team consists of four or five students. Each had to prepare a research paper on an Alaska wildlife species under environmental pressure and include a plan for the species' recovery and an analysis of how the recovery would affect local communities. The papers are reviewed by scientists. The tournament, open to the public at no charge, begins at 8 a.m. t…

title: Volunteers check out creeks' health
summary: If a batch of toxic chemicals were to spill someday into one of Anchorage's creeks, scientists would want to know how the water quality was before the accident occurred. What kind of contaminants were found there before? And how does water quality in certain creeks change with the seasons? That's where volunteer monitors from the Anchorage Waterways Council step in. Over the past three years, volunteers from the nonprofit have scooped buckets of water regularly from creeks throughout the municipality. Once a month in winter and twice a month in summer, the volunteers run nine tests on the water samples using protocols approved by the Environmental Protection Agency. Those records are kept on file, and what is slowly emerging from the database is a portrait of the health of Anchorage's stre…

title: Income tax could cost 3,500 jobs
summary: Juneau -- The average Alaskan would pay about 3 percent of his or her gross income to the state under Gov. Tony Knowles' proposed income tax measure, an economist told a House committee on Tuesday. While the tax would take less of a bite from Alaskans than would a statewide sales tax, it could also result in about 3,500 lost jobs, said Scott Goldsmith, an economist with the University of Alaska Anchorage. Goldsmith testified before the House State Affairs Committee, which is considering several competing tax plans to close the state's budget deficit. Committee Chairman John Coghill, R-North Pole, said he plans to use the testimony to fashion bills to tax income and sales in an effort to spark debate. He is also considering a measure that uses the Alaska Permanent Fund. "Those are the three…

Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 22, 2002) Bekki Spencer, a city employee who helped Wasilla apply for its Tree City USA award, describes the city's plan for the Iditapark. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Grant to help fund Bush sewer project FLUSH: Knowles aims to make honey buckets obsolete. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 23, 2002) The latest installment in an eight-year drive to extend safe water and sewer systems to rural Alaska arrived on Earth Day -- a $23.5 million grant from the U.S. Department of Agriculture. The federal agency's Alaska director for rural development, Bill Allen, delivered the goods in a sunny, and subtle, afternoon ceremony at Westchester Lagoon. Besides Allen and Michele Brown, commissioner of the state Department of Environmental Conservation, only a handful of people and a dozen Canada geese were on hand to observe. The agriculture money, along with another federal grant expected this year from the Environmental Protection Agency, wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Kodiak drafts bear plan MANAGEMENT Group makes 270 recommendations for handling 3,000 animals. By Mary Pemberton The Associated Press (Published: April 22, 2002) After 20 years of trying, the state has come up with the first comprehensive plan for managing the world's largest brown bears. The plan makes 270 recommendations for managing the approximately 3,000 brown bears that roam the 5,000 square miles of the Kodiak archipelago, a group of three large islands and numerous smaller ones stretching southwest of Anchorage, said Larry Van Daele, an Alaska Department of Fish and Game wildlife biologist. Kodiak not only has the largest brown bears in the world but has one of the densest populations. Only a few other remote areas of Alaska and Russia have similar numbers of bears. About a y…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Vote sends no message SUBSISTENCE Positions of Anchorage lawmakers remain unchanged. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 21, 2002) Anchorage's subsistence advisory vote earlier this month was meant to send a message to lawmakers: Let the people vote on subsistence. But three weeks later, it doesn't appear to have changed the minds of key Anchorage legislators. Citywide, 72 percent of voters said yes, and the measure was approved by at least 67 percent within every Anchorage legislative district. Even so, positions on subsistence hunting and fishing rights and preferences run deep. A number of subsistence alternatives are afloat, and the city ballot did not express a preference for any particular solution. "The only thing it changed was it led the governor to h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Black bears on prowl in Juneau SIGHTINGS As bruins awaken, residents are cautioned to monitor their garbage. The Associated Press (Published: April 1, 2002) Juneau -- The bears of Juneau are waking from their winter slumbers about the same time new laws concerning bears and garbage are going into effect. A black bear cub was spotted napping on a warm compost pile Wednesday afternoon at Mile 17.5 Glacier Highway. Another bear was seen late Thursday night on the side of Mendenhall Loop Road at Nancy Street. Those sightings were the first reported since four bears were spotted in December. The black bear cub appeared in the yard of Bill Legere, general manager of KTOO FM and TV. "It was on its own. It wasn't an infant but very small, about the size of a Labrador retriever. It wandered i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Proposed rail, ferry link hotly opposed BUDGET: Measure would combine transportation systems and add state land. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: February 27, 2002) Juneau -- State Sen. Jerry Ward proposes to help solve the state ferry system's budget problems by coupling it with the Alaska Railroad and giving the new system 500,000 acres of state land. But neither ferry system nor railroad officials like the idea. Bill O'Leary, vice president of finance for the Alaska Railroad Corp., told the Senate Transportation Committee on Tuesday the bill could spell disaster for the railroad and the Alaska businesses it serves. And Bob Doll, director of the state Department of Transportation's Southeast region, said the measure won't do anything to solve the ferry system's immed…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Oil pipeline shooting trial gets started CHARGES: Daniel Lewis faces firearms count in federal court. The Associated Press (Published: February 26, 2002) Fairbanks -- A federal trial began Monday for a man accused of shooting the trans-Alaska oil pipeline last year and spilling more than 285,000 gallons of crude oil. A grand jury indicted Daniel Carson Lewis, 37, on Oct. 18 on a charge of unlawful possession of a firearm by a felon. If convicted, Lewis could be sentenced to up to 10 years in prison and ordered to pay a $250,000 fine on the federal charge. He also faces state charges. Federal prosecutor Karen Loeffler told the 12-member jury in U.S. District Court that she will present evidence that Lewis possessed a rifle after being convicted of eight felonies between 1986 and 1994.…

title: Slides close Hatcher Pass
summary: A weekend storm dumped more than 4 feet of snow on the mountains around Hatcher Pass, setting up three avalanches that closed the road Saturday. The snowfall totaled about 56 inches on the east side of the popular recreation area. Multiple slides were reported, said Dale Bingham, state parks Mat-Su area superintendent. Three avalanches slid across Hatcher Pass Road. One just uphill from the Motherlode Lodge buried an 800-foot stretch of road 25 feet deep, said Kurt Devon, state Transportation Department maintenance superintendent. Though the slide swept across the road, some of the snow also flowed down the road, picking the path of least resistance, he said. Avalanches, he said, "were kicking off everywhere." Two smaller slides buried stretches of road farther north toward Independence Mi…

title: Alaska fat on federal funding
summary: Alaska had been weaning itself from federal spending for decades. But six years ago, that long-term trend rebounded, increasing the influence of the U.S. government on the state's economy. Federal spending increased steadily from the 1960s to the mid-1990s, but other sectors of the economy grew faster, and federal dollars made up a relatively smaller and smaller part of the state's economic pie. Then natural resource industries such as logging, fishing and mining leveled off in the 1990s, said University of Alaska Anchorage economist Scott Goldsmith, and federal funding picked back up. The impact of federal funds on the state's economy is a little difficult to unravel, since they come with strings attached, but it is unquestionably significant, analysts say. In 2000, federal spending -- on…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Juneau forecasts record cruise ship visitor season 700,000: Post-attack fears actually benefit U.S. destinations. The Associated Press (Published: April 25, 2002) Juneau -- The Juneau Convention and Visitors Bureau is predicting a record number of cruise ship passengers will visit Juneau this season. Based on the bureau's pre-season projections, more than 700,000 passengers are expected to stop in the city, contrary to early concerns by the state's tourism industry about travel fears stemming from the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. The cruise season kicks off Tuesday with the arrival of Norwegian Cruise Line's Norwegian Sky and ends Sept. 25 with the departure of Celebrity Cruises' Mercury. By summer's end, 718,633 cruise ship passengers and 315,038 crew members are scheduled to visit o…

title: 20 tons of falling steel flatten Subaru
summary: A 20-ton load of steel beams broke loose from a flatbed trailer Wednesday morning on Muldoon Road, crushing a red Subaru station wagon and injuring a passenger. John Fleming, 32, of Wasilla was taken to Alaska Regional Hospital with possible broken ribs, police said. He was treated and released. The chain of events that ended with the smashed car started at 10:47 a.m. on busy Muldoon Road. James Simmema, 52, was driving a tractor-trailer south to deliver the green, steel I-beams to the Muldoon Mall at East 12th Avenue and Muldoon when a smaller truck cut him off, clipping the flatbed's front bumper, police said. Wasilla resident David Nevers, who was behind the smaller truck in the Subaru, saw the accident and pulled alongside the trailer to tell Simmema his bumper was bent. The driver of …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fish observers to stay on board 2007: NMFS extends rule for officials on groundfish vessles The Associated Press (Published: April 16, 2002) Federal fishery managers on Monday voted to extend through 2007 a regulation requiring observers on groundfish vessels. The rule had been due to expire at the end of this year. The North Pacific Fishery Management Council also gave the National Marine Fisheries Service authority to station observers aboard the vessels and at fish plants that process North Pacific groundfish or halibut. The change will strengthen the relationship between the NMFS and the observer companies to ensure sufficient management controls, according to the agency. NMFS observers could be sent out in addition to or instead of contracted groundfish observers. Allowing NMFS …

title: Good teachers getting hard to find
summary: By the time it finished hiring in 1992, the Anchorage School District had more than 3,000 leftover applications from people wanting to teach here. Today, Teresa Johnson, director of staffing and recruitment, has 569 spare applications. And the applicants are not as qualified, Johnson said Tuesday, testifying to the House Special Committee on Education. The committee, chaired by Rep. Con Bunde, R-Anchorage, visited the Kodiak School District on Monday, and the Matanuska-Susitna Borough and Anchorage School Districts on Tuesday. Members were headed to the Northwest Arctic Borough School District in Kotzebue today, their final stop on a road trip to gather ideas about what the committee calls a continuing shortage of qualified teachers in Alaska. People may hear "teacher shortage" and think o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Panel tightens abortion funding SENATE: Finance Committee approves bill to redefine eligibility rules. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: April 24, 2002) Juneau -- The Senate Finance Committee approved a bill Tuesday tightening the rules for state funding of abortions. Senate Bill 364 is a response to some lawmakers' frustration with a Supreme Court decision requiring the state's Medicaid program to pay for medically necessary abortions for poor women. The court ruled last year that the state may not deny funding for medically advised abortions for poor women if it provides other pregnancy-related services for poor women. Sen. Pete Kelly, R-Fairbanks, said state regulations interpret medical necessity too loosely. Opponents of the bill said Kelly's attempt to tighten the…

title: Don Young directs land to hometown
summary: Fairbanks -- The Fort Yukon village corporation will receive a parcel of unused military property because Rep. Don Young didn't want a federal agency to take possession of it. Young, whose Alaska home is in Fort Yukon, said Wednesday that he put language in a federal spending bill last year to direct the land in the center of town to Gwitchyaa Zhee Corp. If he hadn't, the land could have reverted to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which manages Yukon Flats National Wildlife Refuge surrounding the village. The Fish and Wildlife Service already has enough land, Young said. And the village needs the land for a new power plant, he said. Young said he was afraid that the agency would seek to strike a land exchange deal with the Native corporation. He envisioned the agency offering Gwitchyaa…

title: Trees topple in city's war against wildfires
summary: The Spencer Loop, one of Anchorage's premier park trails, is being logged, the latest battleground in the city's war against potential wildfires. And this is just the beginning, city and federal land managers said. In the past four weeks, next to Hilltop Ski Area, more than 40 percent of the loop has been cleared of beetle-killed spruce trees to 50 feet on both sides of the trail. The logging is "low impact," as these things go, said Sue Rodman, an Anchorage Fire Department forester and a project manager. A contractor is removing only dead or dying trees, ones that would fall to the ground in a few years anyway. But the trees are large and the equipment is heavy, and the project's effect on the park is striking. The trail's dirt bed has been loosened by machinery and log dragging. Piles of…

title: Summit to debate the future of privacy
summary: The state Department of Motor Vehicles keeps a computer file on you. So does your health insurance company. And that online book store? They have your number and may be selling it to other businesses. Government agencies and private companies collect and can share all sorts of information on individuals, a process that has become faster, easier and more common thanks to the Internet. How it's done and whether it should be regulated will be the topic of a three-day conference on privacy that begins tonight in Anchorage. A privacy expert from Microsoft, an FBI agent, the state corrections commissioner, a technology specialist with the University of Alaska and attorneys with British Petroleum and AT&T will be among the panelists at the Privacy Summit for Alaskans. The opening session this eve…

summary: | | | Donley repeats call for budget cuts DEFICIT:Republican says spending limits are key to bridging gap. Tony Knowles introduced a $400 million tax plan to close Alaska's fiscal gap, Senate Finance Committee Co-Chairman Dave Donley repeated his calls for budget cuts and a limit on spending before any major new taxes are enacted. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Mailer targets Whittle MUD: GOP letter says Assembly hopeful smeared' Paul Bauer. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 20, 2002) The Republican Party's mailer this week supporting East Anchorage Assembly candidate Paul Bauer Jr. has added an increasingly partisan edge to what the Anchorage city charter says shall be a nonpartisan city election. The campaign letter claims Bauer was "smeared" recently by his opponent, Brian Whittle. Whittle says he had been responding to a Bauer attack. The candidates are headed for a run-off election May 7. The Republicans gave Bauer $1,000 on April 8. The Democratic Party has not sent Whittle cash, but its executive director helped him with fund-raisers. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: April 20, 2002) Healy plant gets energy bill boost The U.S. Senate on Thursday approved an amendment to the energy bill that would authorize the U.S. energy secretary to grant up to a $125 million loan to stoke the troubled Healy "clean coal" power plant near Denali National Park and Preserve. The $278 million, state-owned plant ran sporadically for two years until late 1999. That year, Golden Valley Electric Association -- the Fairbanks-based electric company that was supposed to buy the electricity from the government-funded plant -- declared the plant's experimental combustors unworkable. U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski, R-Alaska, proposed the loan amendment. The $125 million would allow the state to convert the coal plant's experimental combustors to …

title: The great speckled bird
summary: Caswell Lake -- Fred Virrazzi was sitting at his computer in Carteret, N.J., when he got the big news: A rare woodpecker had taken up residence at a remote cabin near Talkeetna. Two weeks later -- having waited until his wife was out of town -- Virrazzi and fellow birder Chip Krilowicz boarded a plane for the 3,500-mile trip north to Alaska. The hunt was on, and these two were not alone. From across America, the birders were coming, from as far away as Leesburg, Va., Tolleson, Ariz., and Friendswood, Texas. They all wanted a glimpse of this unusual woodpecker, which looks a lot like Alaska's black-and-white hairy woodpecker, though it appears its underbelly has been dipped in bold red paint. That red underbelly branded this woodpecker as a world traveler. It was at least 2,000 miles from w…

title: Teenager missing since Monday police welcome information
summary: ANCHORAGE Teenager missing since Monday police welcome information Anchorage Police are searching for a missing 13-year-old girl. Echo Knapp was last seen on Monday, when she was dropped off at the Royal Roller Rink, 6411 DeBarr Road, with a friend. She is 5 feet, 2 inches tall, weighs 150 pounds, and has sandy hair and blue eyes. Anyone who has seen her or knows her whereabouts is asked to call the police at 786-8900. -- Anchorage Daily News KENAI Flatbed truck strikes natural gas pipeline building; no leak detected Officials with Marathon Oil say no natural gas leaked after a flatbed truck left the Kenai Spur Highway on Monday afternoon and struck a building that houses a pipeline valve at North Miller Road. title: Army sued over range cleanup
summary: Three environmental groups and the Chickaloon tribe have sued to stop the Army from shelling the Eagle River Flats firing range and force it to clean up the unexploded munitions. The military has worked for the past seven years to rid the Fort Richardson firing range of white phosphorous, a substance used in some munitions that was found to kill ducks and other waterfowl. The plaintiffs in the lawsuit commended that effort but want more to be done to make the wetlands next to Cook Inlet safe for humans and wildlife. Unexploded ordnance is scattered throughout the 2,500-acre range, surrounding the lower reach of Eagle River. Although no data links the munitions with harm to the environment, the plaintiffs argue the rounds contain heavy metals and other dangerous chemicals that could leach i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | British Columbia lifts salmon farm ban REACTION: Knowles pans decision to OK new aquaculture operations. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 1, 2002) Alaska Gov. Tony Knowles ripped a decision Thursday by officials in British Columbia to lift a 1995 ban on new salmon farms along the Canadian province's coastline. Knowles said salmon farms, banned in Alaska, can create pollution, disease and escapes of nonnative Atlantic salmon into the wild, possibly threatening Alaska's wild salmon runs. "Alaska wisely took action to ban this practice a decade ago, along with steps to protect and grow our populations of wild salmon for commercial, sport and subsistence uses," Knowles said. He urged British Columbia officials to reconsider their decision "to allow unchecked expans…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BP's gas pipeline dreams go on hold FEASIBILITY: Low natural gas prices keep plans at bay for now. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 16, 2002) BP remains interested in a proposed Alaska natural gas pipeline to the Lower 48, but the estimated $15 billion and $20 billion is not financially feasible at today's gas prices, said chief executive John Browne. Speaking to analysts in New York on Thursday, Browne said the pipeline "is not a very good project at all" because of current prices and competing energy supplies. The pipeline dream, which has been around for 25 years, was revived as gas prices rose to $10 per million BTU 14 months ago. Prices have since fallen and on Friday were $2.21. While Browne said BP is still considering the project, the message appare…

title: Money flies in battle over prison
summary: Soldotna -- The marquee issue on Tuesday's Kenai Peninsula ballot -- whether to proceed with the state's first private prison -- has drawn more than $200,000 in campaign contributions so far. Most of the money has wound up in newspaper ink and radio spots, either touting the proposed prison as a economic boon, or, conversely, calling it a threat to the region's quality of life. Three-quarters of the money, about $160,000, has been contributed by private prison backers, such as Cornell Corrections, Kenai Natives Association, VECO Inc. and Neeser Construction Inc., according to reports filed with the Alaska Public Offices Commission. Cornell, in partnership with KNA, likely would operate the prison. VECO and Neeser are also involved in the project. Roughly $50,000 has been raised by private …

title: Natives fear Indian Affairs plan would hurt villages
summary: Alaska's tribes joined others across the country this week in opposing a Bush administration proposal to reorganize the Bureau of Indian Affairs. The reorganization was proposed last November by Interior Secretary Gale Norton as a way for the government to gain better control of trust accounts mismanaged in the past by the BIA. The government has been sued by Indians claiming as much as $10 billion in royalties owed to individual Indians was lost because of faulty bookkeeping and theft. But opponents of the reorganization plan, which would remove trust operations from the BIA, say it could lead to dissolving the main federal agency dealing with Native Americans. They also say the government should have consulted with tribes before announcing the huge overhaul. "There's a sense that this be…

title: Skiers guard secrets on remote ins 'n' outs
summary: Winter still had the higher elevations of the Kenai Mountains locked in its grip in June when Jerry Dixon got the call reporting fresh ski tracks cutting down 5,265-foot Mount Alice near Seward. Harold Faust and George Peck had tracked Dixon down on his cellphone. They couldn't wait to give him the news. Alice is a forbidding peak, one the three skiers had spent years scheming how to get up and ski down. They knew the news would get Dixon's goat. Dixon couldn't believe it. "I thought maybe a couple of 20-year-olds had gotten off the boat and poached it," he said. The tracks signaled a new chapter for backcountry skiing in the Seward area. Dixon and a small cadre of locals, including Faust and Peck, were no longer exploring the nearby mountains on skis by themselves. The Seward area has a b…

title: Winter rains soak slopes, boost slides
summary: The big sloppy storm that struck Southcentral Alaska during Christmas week whomped Turnagain Arm with almost 10 inches of ice-cold rain while dangerously loading many mountain slopes already layered with unstable snow. The effects were dramatic and wet -- flooding parking lots in the Portage area and triggering dozens of avalanches along the Seward Highway from Bird to Moose Pass. "It wasn't just the rain, it was the wind," said Mike Miller, owner of Big Game Alaska Wildlife Center in Portage. "It was like one of those car washes where you can sit in your car and go through the machine." With rain soaking up to the 2,000-foot level in the Kenai and Chugach mountains, the amount of moisture in the cold, poorly bonded snowpack doubled in some locations, creating what avalanche expert Doug Fe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Sea marshals drop in on Alaska SECURITY: Like planes, ships randomly carry extra protection. By Anne Marie Tavella Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 11, 2002) The Polar Eagle, a liquefied natural gas tanker, came to port in Nikiski on Wednesday carrying unexpected passengers on deck: Coast Guard sea marshals to ensure the ship's security. Similar to the federal sky marshal's program, the sea marshals program started shortly after Sept. 11 to increase security on vessels in U.S. waters. It began in San Francisco and has since spread to other Coast Guard units in the country, including in Los Angeles, San Diego and Alaska, said Lt. Cmdr. Jim Robertson of the Marine Safety Office in Anchorage. The armed marshals patrol the ships looking for security risks and serve as a deterrent…

title: DEC staffers raise concerns in survey
summary: Low morale, funding woes and weak leadership are among concerns raised from employees of the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation in a survey by a government worker advocacy group. Some employees also said they're not as effective because big industries like oil have too much influence. And at times regulators have been reassigned for doing their jobs "too well" on controversial projects, according to more than half of those surveyed. Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility conducted the confidential survey at the request of some DEC employees. The Washington, D.C.-based nonprofit, which is funded by foundations, membership fees and legal awards, works to improve environmental accountability in government agencies. DEC Commissioner Michele Brown downplayed the results,…

title: Court nullifies Tongass logging permits
summary: San Francisco -- A federal appeals court nullified as many as 100 logging permits Wednesday, a decision mainly affecting tree harvesters in the Tongass National Forest in southeastern Alaska. The 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals said environmentalists and others should have been given a legal forum to protest new rules allowing the companies to produce more wood waste in estuaries and coastal zones than previously permitted. At issue are the federal permits to run so-called "logging transfer facilities."
